(**2026-07-13 — prod lineage restored + the #829/#830 pair merged.** The lineage outage was ours: the dbt connection's ADLS SAS **expired 6 Jul**, so every 10-min poll failed `ClientAuthenticationError` and DataQ never read `manifest.json` — the dbt builds stayed green and kept publishing artifacts nobody consumed. Marquez was a red herring; the live path is the #759 dbt-manifest one. Re-minted the SAS (1y), re-ran the build, and the chain fired end to end (poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → `lineage_edges` → graph; the trigger-binding also ran the bound suite 4/4 green). **Two systemic defects fall out, still open on [#828](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/828):** a failing poll has **no user-visible signal** and the UI's empty-lineage state is indistinguishable from "this asset has no upstreams"; and `_POLL_LOOKBACK` is 15 min, so once auth was fixed the poll succeeded and still recorded **nothing** — every build produced during the outage had aged out and is stranded in the artifact store, recoverable only by re-running the producer. [#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Failure semantics #122: reader/engine refusals + unexpected exceptions → operational `error` results (classified messages, never raw text). The sample redactor gains a comparison branch — suffix-stripped policy matching so `pii_columns: [email]` masks `email_src`+`email_tgt`, unknown columns keep default-mask. **#793 comparison engine shipped ([#808](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/808))** — `datasources/comparison.py`, the FDC record-grain diff port (ADR 0014 §2: engine yes, app no): key-joined outer-merge buckets (matched / mismatched / additional-in-source / additional-in-target) with per-column mismatch counts, typed `DuplicateKeyError` (FDC silently drop_duplicates-ed — pairing must never be ambiguous) + `ComparisonInputError`, NA-safe nullable-`string` comparison (null==null matches; | ✅ | [#849](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/849) **SECURITY: DataQ PATs were logged in PLAINTEXT to App Insights on every PAT-authenticated request.** Found live in prod telemetry while chasing an unrelated outage. `fastapi_azure_auth` logs the raw bearer when a JWT decode fails — and a PAT is never a valid JWT, so it failed *by construction*. Worse, `Security(azure_scheme)` is a FastAPI **dependency**: it resolves BEFORE `get_current_user`'s body, so the "PAT is tried first by prefix" ordering the code documents was **never actually first**. Every PAT request handed the credential to a JWT validator, which logged it in the clear, and only then fell through to the PAT path — which succeeded, so the request worked and nothing looked wrong. **A PAT is a live bearer credential**: anyone with App Insights read could authenticate as its owner, including the workspace-admin. Present since #613. **Both prod PATs revoked** (verified 401); re-minted only after the fix deployed. Fixed in two layers — the PAT never reaches the validator (`_PatAwareAzureScheme`), AND the logger scrubs bare credentials (`dq_live_…`/JWT/`Bearer …`) plus credential header keys. **Layer 2 is the architectural point: the call site that leaked was NOT OURS** — CLAUDE.md §10 already says redact at the logger, and grepping our own code for "places we log tokens" would never have found this, because we don't log it; a dependency does. (The MCP path had it right — it checks the prefix *before* calling the verifier.) Tests drive the REAL pipeline (a helper-only test stays green if the processor chain is reordered), mint a REAL token (a prefix literal would miss a changed alphabet), and intercept the LIBRARY's `__call__` (the buggy version also returned None — *after* logging — so asserting our return value would have passed on the bug); verified failing when redaction is removed. En route: I pasted the real token into the test file and betterleaks caught it in **git history** — a revoked credential is still a credential ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#852](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/852) **The exporter was amplifying its own logs — 19,206 traces in 30 minutes (~10/sec).** `azure.core`'s HTTP policy logs a request AND response line for every SDK call, including the OTel exporter's own uploads to App Insights: upload → logged → reaches root → re-enters the export bridge → uploaded → logged again. The existing loop-breaker detached `opentelemetry` + `azure.monitor.opentelemetry` but **missed `azure.core`**, and unlike those, this loop doesn't need the backend to be down. The real cost isn't volume — **the noise drowned the signal**: while diagnosing a prod outage, a query for the app's own `orchestration_poll_completed` returned nothing usable, buried under thousands of `"Response status: 200"` lines. Observability that

| ✅ | [#849](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/849) **SECURITY: DataQ PATs were logged in PLAINTEXT to App Insights on every PAT-authenticated request.** Found live in prod telemetry while chasing an unrelated outage. `fastapi_azure_auth` logs the raw bearer when a JWT decode fails — and a PAT is never a valid JWT, so it failed *by construction*. Worse, `Security(azure_scheme)` is a FastAPI **dependency**: it resolves BEFORE `get_current_user`'s body, so the "PAT is tried first by prefix" ordering the code documents was **never actually first**. Every PAT request handed the credential to a JWT validator, which logged it in the clear, and only then fell through to the PAT path — which succeeded, so the request worked and nothing looked wrong. **A PAT is a live bearer credential**: anyone with App Insights read could authenticate as its owner, including the workspace-admin. Present since #613. **Both prod PATs revoked** (verified 401); re-minted only after the fix deployed. Fixed in two layers — the PAT never reaches the validator (`_PatAwareAzureScheme`), AND the logger scrubs bare credentials (`dq_live_…`/JWT/`Bearer …`) plus credential header keys. **Layer 2 is the architectural point: the call site that leaked was NOT OURS** — CLAUDE.md §10 already says redact at the logger, and grepping our own code for "places we log tokens" would never have found this, because we don't log it; a dependency does. (The MCP path had it right — it checks the prefix *before* calling the verifier.) Tests drive the REAL pipeline (a helper-only test stays green if the processor chain is reordered), mint a REAL token (a prefix literal would miss a changed alphabet), and intercept the LIBRARY's `__call__` (the buggy version also returned None — *after* logging — so asserting our return value would have passed on the bug); verified failing when redaction is removed. En route: I pasted the real token into the test file and betterleaks caught it in **git history** — a revoked credential is still a credential ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#852](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/852) **The exporter was amplifying its own logs — 19,206 traces in 30 minutes (~10/sec).** `azure.core`'s HTTP policy logs a request AND response line for every SDK call, including the OTel exporter's own uploads to App Insights: upload → logged → reaches root → re-enters the export bridge → uploaded → logged again. The existing loop-breaker detached `opentelemetry` + `azure.monitor.opentelemetry` but **missed `azure.core`**, and unlike those, this loop doesn't need the backend to be down. The real cost isn't volume — **the noise drowned the signal**: while diagnosing a prod outage, a query for the app's own `orchestration_poll_completed` returned nothing usable, buried under thousands of `"Response status: 200"` lines. Observability that hides the event you're looking for is worse than none, because you trust it. Silenced at INFO (not detached — a genuine transport WARNING still gets through) ([PR #851](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/851)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#845](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/845) **The lineage graph DEFEATED the no-leak 404** (found in prod: Olivia clicked a downstream mart → "asset not found"). The dead link was the symptom; the defect was that ADR 0034 decision 5 promises an asset outside your grants is 404-**no-leak**, and the endpoint is built so it cannot confirm such an asset exists — while the graph handed a non-grantee its **name, namespace, env and monitored-status** one click earlier. The guarantee was buying nothing. A neighbour behind the grant boundary is now **redacted, not omitted**: an anonymous node, drawn but not clickable, with a count ("1 connected asset is outside your access"). Dropping it would assert "nothing consumes this table" — false, and the same confident-empty-state lie #828/#823 were about; **we don't fix a leak by shipping a lie**. Redaction is server-side: a name hidden in CSS has still crossed the wire ([PR #848](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/848)) | 🔒 ops |
| ✅ | [#846](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/846) **Browse omitted suite-less assets — the list and the detail endpoint disagreed about what exists** (user: "under Snowflake ANALYTICS it shows 1 asset, there are at least 2"). The list filtered `Asset.id IN (SELECT suite.asset_id …)`, so a lineage-discovered table was unfindable **even for an admin**, though `get_visible_asset` explicitly hands admins those orphans. Pulling the thread showed the boundary was drawn in the wrong place: **a suite-less asset was treated as "outside your grants", but redaction protects a GRANT** — an asset nobody has granted is protected by nothing (no suites ⇒ no runs, results or samples; only its *name* was withheld, which lineage reveals anywa

result.output: 27:| **Open issues** | **33** in the **`v1.1 Backlog`** milestone (76 open repo-wide) — `gh issue list --milestone 'v1.1 Backlog' --state open`; the population is stated here because the figure has silently drifted before, so **re-count it, don't decrement it**. (**2026-07-13 — prod lineage restored + the #829/#830 pair merged.** The lineage outage was ours: the dbt connection's ADLS SAS **expired 6 Jul**, so every 10-min poll failed `ClientAuthenticationError` and DataQ never read `manifest.json` — the dbt builds stayed green and kept publishing artifacts nobody consumed. Marquez was a red herring; the live path is the #759 dbt-manifest one. Re-minted the SAS (1y), re-ran the build, and the chain fired end to end (poll → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → `lineage_edges` → graph; the trigger-binding also ran the bound suite 4/4 green). **Two systemic defects fall out, still open on [#828](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/828):** a failing poll has **no user-visible signal** and the UI's empty-lineage state is indistinguishable from "this asset has no upstreams"; and `_POLL_LOOKBACK` is 15 min, so once auth was fixed the poll succeeded and still recorded **nothing** — every build produced during the outage had aged out and is stranded in the artifact store, recoverable only by re-running the producer. [#823](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/823) narrowed to the still-unproven Marquez pull path. Shipped alongside: [#829](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/829) — a suite was **unshareable from a phone** (the Add button painted at x=407 on a 390px viewport) — and [#830](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/830) — the assets UI printed the raw OL namespace (an Iceberg DSN) instead of a human label. **Both agentic reviews earned their keep:** on #829 the review caught that the new e2e spec was **worthless** (`toBeInViewport()` defaults to `ratio: 0`, and 71% of the broken button was on screen — it passed on the bug); on #830 it caught a **missed fifth render site** (the dashboard) and that the label still **leaked the DB username** when a catalog URI had no path, which made the commit message's own claim false.) (**user follow-up batch #801–#805 all closed 2026-07-12** — the five UI/ops follow-ups filed off the ADR-0015 session: mobile overlay nav ([#817](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/817)), hierarchical asset browse ([#818](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/818)), asset-health split into connection-vs-data-quality axes ([#819](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/819)), the left-to-right lineage graph ([#820](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/820)), and the Marquez local verification + runbook correction ([#821](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/821)). Four latent bugs surfaced and were fixed en route: a run that evaluated nothing read as a green "Passing"; a `cancelled` run rolled up green on **both** health axes; an asset→asset navigation showed the *previous* asset's data (nothing could reach it before the graph's clickable nodes); and the demo seed had **no lineage at all**, so a fresh install could not show the feature. **#813 closed via [#814](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/814)** — weekly `/security-scan` OWASP spot-check found the MCP `create_check`/`update_check` tools had no Pydantic layer, so `name`/`expectation_type` could exceed the REST 256/128 bounds and hit a raw Postgres `StringDataRightTruncation` instead of a clean 422; pushed `check_service.validate_lengths` (now public) into both, extended to `suite_io_service.import_suite`'s direct `Check(...)` construction too (agentic review caught that same-class gap pre-merge). Same session: 13 open CodeQL code-scanning alerts triaged — 10 dismissed as false-positive/by-design (module-level cache globals, a documented lazy-import cycle, an intentional test canary), 3 genuine (`side-effect-in-assert` ×2, `unnecessary-lambda`) fixed in [#815](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/815). **#799 comparison:columns + tolerance + optimization shipped (this PR)** — FDC's column grain as `comparison:columns` (per-column matched/mismatched/additional-per-side VALUE buckets, dropna parity, same sample shape → redaction/report reuse), numeric `config.tolerance` {absolute, relative} on both grains (the #808-review float32 finding — one-sided NULLs never tolerance-equal), and the shared keys+positions alignment pass (×1.52 on the 1M-row benchmark, bucket parity asserted; integer keys now render "1" not "1.0" in samples); catalog fixture regenerated, per-column breakdown + tolerance fields in the UI. · **ADR [0015](adr/0015-two-connection-comparison-check-model.md) written + merged ([#790](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/790), 2026-07-12)** — the two-connection comparison check model: the suite stays single-connection and supplies the **target under test**; a `comparison` check adds one **source** ref. Closes the design gate reserved since ADR 0014; build set [#791](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/791)–[#795](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/795) filed on W3. **#795 comparison UI + report download shipped (this PR) — the ADR 0015 build is COMPLETE (#791–#795, five PRs in one session 2026-07-12)**: side-by-side check editor (source pane = connection picker + table-or-SQL per type + keys; target pane locked to the suite's connection — the §1 invariant made visible; common options below), RunDetail bucket counts + redacted sample tables + on-demand CSV/XLSX report download (derived server-side from the redacted buckets via new `comparison_report.py` + `openpyxl` (MIT, pinned), never stored), comparison-safe CheckEdit (name+thresholds only), catalog entry + regenerated GX-contract fixture, feature-matrix row; Playwright comparison spec green against the live local stack (which also re-validated #797 — the stale api container 429'd the suite until recreated). **#794 comparison run path shipped ([#809](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/809))** — `services/comparison_run.py` builds a per-run executor bound to the suite's resolved target (source side = the check's ref + `config.source` through `resolve_target`/batch materialization or a query spec; target side = the run's materialized table or `config.target_query`), reads both via the #792 reader, diffs with the #793 engine, and maps buckets → `CheckOutcome` (mismatch-% → `metric_value` under ADR 0016 banding; counts → `observed_value`; capped samples → `sample_failures`). `_run_outcomes` routes comparison checks to the injected executor (None → per-check `error`, callers stay safe); worker builds it only when the suite has comparison checks. Failure semantics #122: reader/engine refusals + unexpected exceptions → operational `error` results (classified messages, never raw text). The sample redactor gains a comparison branch — suffix-stripped policy matching so `pii_columns: [email]` masks `email_src`+`email_tgt`, unknown columns keep default-mask. **#793 comparison engine shipped ([#808](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/808))** — `datasources/comparison.py`, the FDC record-grain diff port (ADR 0014 §2: engine yes, app no): key-joined outer-merge buckets (matched / mismatched / additional-in-source / additional-in-target) with per-column mismatch counts, typed `DuplicateKeyError` (FDC silently drop_duplicates-ed — pairing must never be ambiguous) + `ComparisonInputError`, NA-safe nullable-`string` comparison (null==null matches; a literal "nan" ≠ NULL — fixes the FDC astype(str) quirk), pairwise numeric canonicalization (numpy NULL-int→float64 "10.0" vs Arrow "10" can't fabricate mismatches; dup-key detection on normalized keys, samples echo original values), mismatch-% badness scalar over the logical-row union, capped JSON-clean sample buckets. **#792 DatasetReader seam shipped ([#807](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/807))** — `services/dataset_reader.py`: per-datasource capped DataFrame reads behind profiler-style dict dispatch (SQL via the profiler engine plumbing, flat-file object read, `pyiceberg` scan; orchestration types 422), COUNT/scan-metadata preflight + post-read race guard + typed `DatasetTooLargeError` (fail-fast, never truncation), read-time read-only re-validation of query specs, `COMPARISON_MAX_ROWS` setting (default 100k, per-check `config.max_rows` override) documented in deploy/README. **#791 schema + authoring slice shipped ([#798](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/798))** — `checks.source_connection_id` FK + kind⇔presence CHECK (migration `b7c8d9e0f1a2`, up+down tested), author-time validation (source spec via `resolve_target`, read-only per-side SQL per ADR 0019, join keys, orchestration sources rejected), RESTRICT delete guard (409 `connection_in_use`), export/import `(name, env)` round-trip, version snapshots, and the run-path guard (a comparison check yields a per-check `error` result until the #794 runner — never fails its siblings' run). User follow-up batch filed to `v1.1 Backlog` (2026-07-12): [#799](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/799) comparison:columns + optimization pass · [#800](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/800) Astronomer verification · [#801](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/801) mobile overlay nav · [#802](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/802) hierarchical asset browse · [#803](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/803) asset health split · [#804](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/804) Marquez in harness · [#805](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/805) lineage graph view · [#806](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/806) stale-nosec chore. Same session: **#796 CI-E2E 429 regression fixed via [#797](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/797)** — the #783 limiter throttled the tokenless dev-bypass Playwright suite (whole suite = one per-IP `unauth` bucket); diagnosed from the report artifact ("Too many requests" rendered in the failing pages); `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=false` in the CI E2E job + compose dev default. · **#725 request rate limiting shipped (PR #783)** — app-level *innermost* HTTP middleware on every public surface (REST + orchestration webhooks + the `/mcp` mount a route dependency can't reach); fixed-window Redis counter (window index in the key → no EXPIRE race), **no new dep** (`redis` INCR over slowapi/limits), **fail-open**; endpoint classes `webhook`(per-IP) · `default`(per-`sha256(token)`) · `unauth`(per-IP), raw token never keyed/logged; `/healthz`+`OPTIONS` exempt; 429 carries `Retry-After` + `detail.retry_after_seconds`; ADR [0035](adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md); the named hard prereq for the ADR-0032 OTP sign-in (#738). · **#773 nav inversion shipped (this PR)** — G-d phase 4: the dashboard opens with an asset-health lead strip (monitored / need-attention / in-progress, severity-sorted preview, explicit first-200 truncation note), Assets moves to the top of the sidebar, suite/run detail link back to their asset, and admins get an owner-reassignment picker on AssetDetail (mount-gated so the /me-resolution race can't strand it; `/admin/users` never fetched for non-admins — structural + test-pinned). Frontend-only — the owner PATCH backend had already shipped in #772. Same session: **UC dbt lineage live-verified PASS** (real `dbt-databricks` build on the live workspace → manifest v12 → `refresh_dbt_lineage` → correct 2-edge chain; the run-stamped UC asset and the dbt node resolved to the SAME row, provenance preserved; blast radius correct both directions — closes the feature-matrix's "adapter-aware, not live-tested" caveat) and the **AssetDetail CI flake fixed** (#778 via [#780](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/780), 15s budgets). · **#768 check-editor type guidance closed** — the `expect_column_values_to_be_of_type` UX finding from the #767 live schema-drift session: added the expectation to the check-editor catalog with a datasource-tailored `type_` hint (Snowflake: exact SQL dialect type; UC/flat-file/Iceberg: Python value type name — verified per-runner execution engine, not guessed) + a [datasources-checks.md](datasources-checks.md) cheat-sheet; the dry-run preview already surfaced `observed_value` for calibration, so no new plumbing there. **G-d build COMPLETE — #762 LineageProvider/Marquez shipped** (the seam + pull + compose profile; all six G-d issues #596/#757–#762 now closed) · **#761 incidents shipped** (G-d phase 3 — lifecycle+dedup engine, evidence card, publisher payload, grant-derived incident API + AssetDetail panel; #773 nav-inversion filed as phase 4) · **W3 in progress 2026-07-11** — **#767 P1 result-misattribution fixed** ([PR #771](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/771) — GX error-first reorder vs positional zip; found + fixed off the **2026-07-10/11 live-prod-DB test session**: schema-drift 6/6 detected on SF+UC, #759 add/drop-view lineage propagation proven E2E, dbt `artifacts_uri` docs footgun [#769](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/769); filed [#768](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/768) type-calibration UX + [#770](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/770) orphan-asset sweep; leaked UC PAT rotated same-session). **#760 asset view shipped** (G-d phase 2 — `/assets` read API + Assets pages, grant-derived authz, `asset_id` on Suite/RunRead; MCP tool explicitly deferred). Two byte-identical copies of the plain-identifier regex (monitors._IDENT_RE,
profile_service._IDENTIFIER) and two near-line-identical run_monitors
engine/connection/scalar bodies (Snowflake, Unity Catalog) collapse into a
new backend/app/datasources/sql.py:

- SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE / is_sql_identifier — the single allowlist decision;
  callers keep their own error types (MonitorConfigError vs the profiler's
  422). Widening the set now happens everywhere at once.
- run_monitors_over_engine(engine, ...) — the shared open-one-connection →
  evaluate_monitors loop; the runners now differ only in URL/engine
  construction (incl. the SF-only schema fallback, which stays at its call
  site). The caller owns the engine lifecycle — the seam #427 will thread a
  per-run shared engine through.

Behavior unchanged; monitors.py stays connection-free (the engine helper
lazy-imports it, avoiding a cycle).

Fixes #428

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push -u origin refactor/issue-428-shared-sql-ident && gh pr create --title "refactor(datasources): one SQL-identifier allowlist + one run_monitors engine loop (#428)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "## Summary

Fixes #428 (surfaced by the #426 code review; W4 prep — this is the code #592 \`schema_drift\` builds on).

## Changes

- **New \`backend/app/datasources/sql.py\`** — shared SQL-datasource primitives:
  - \`SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE\` / \`is_sql_identifier\`: the one plain-identifier allowlist (Snowflake/Databricks unquoted set). \`monitors._ident\` and \`profile_service.validate_identifier\` both route through it; each keeps its own error type (\`MonitorConfigError\` vs 422 \`ProfileIdentifierInvalidError\`). The regex copies are deleted.
  - \`run_monitors_over_engine(engine, ...)\`: the engine → one connection → \`evaluate_monitors\` scalar loop the SF/UC runners previously duplicated. Runners now differ only in URL/engine construction; the SF-only \`schema or self._config.schema_\` fallback stays at its call site. Caller owns the engine lifecycle — the seam #427 threads a per-run shared engine through next.
- \`monitors.py\` remains connection-free (its documented property); the helper lazy-imports \`evaluate_monitors\` to avoid the cycle.
- Note: the issue's third copy (custom-SQL path) no longer exists — it was refactored away since #426; only the two regex copies remained.

## Acceptance criteria (from #428)

- [x] SQL-identifier validation consolidated to one helper; \`run_monitors\` boilerplate deduped across SQL runners
- [x] Existing monitor tests green

## Testing

- New \`backend/tests/datasources/test_sql.py\`: allowlist accept/reject (incl. non-strings), a pin that BOTH consumers route through the shared decision, and \`run_monitors_over_engine\` over a real sqlite engine (happy path, per-monitor error isolation, connection-failure propagation).
- Monitors / snowflake / unity_catalog / profile_service suites green (207 tests); full backend suite green locally.

Also carries the docs/progress.md ledger flip for the just-merged #872 (#785) + Snapshot recount (next commit).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)"

content.text: While the #873 reviewers run, starting the #726 + #429 design — reading the dispatch surfaces:

result.output:
1	"""Datasource adapter seam.
2	
3	Every datasource (Snowflake now; ADLS / S3 / Unity Catalog later) executes DQ
4	checks behind one ``CheckRunner`` interface that speaks GX-agnostic DTOs. The
5	GX-specific machinery lives entirely inside each adapter, so the run-service and
6	its tests depend only on the types here — never on Great Expectations internals.
7	This is also the seam that lets v1.1 swap GX for DQX on Unity Catalog (CLAUDE.md
8	§5) without rippling into the suite / check / result layer.
9	
10	`CheckSpec` goes in (a check pulled from the DB); `CheckOutcome` comes out, one
11	per check, shaped to map cleanly onto the `results` table columns. Adapters
12	translate GX results into these DTOs; tests provide a fake `CheckRunner` and
13	never touch a live datasource.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	from dataclasses import dataclass
19	from typing import Any, Protocol, runtime_checkable
20	
21	from pydantic import BaseModel
22	
23	
24	@dataclass(frozen=True)
25	class CheckSpec:
26	    """One expectation to evaluate, sourced from a `checks` row.
27	
28	    `expectation_type` is the GX snake_case name (e.g.
29	    ``expect_column_values_to_not_be_null``); `kwargs` are its parameters
30	    (e.g. ``{"column": "id"}``). Adapters own the translation to the concrete
31	    GX expectation class.
32	    """
33	
34	    expectation_type: str
35	    kwargs: dict[str, Any]
36	
37	
38	@dataclass(frozen=True)
39	class CheckOutcome:
40	    """Result of one check, shaped for the `results` table.
41	
42	    `observed_value` / `expected_value` / `sample_failures` land in the
43	    matching JSONB columns. `sample_failures` may contain real data rows, so it
44	    is governed by the retention sweep and must only ever be logged through the
45	    PII-redacting structlog chain.
46	
47	    `errored` marks a check that could not be *evaluated* (the runner caught an
48	    exception while computing it — e.g. it references a missing column), as
49	    opposed to a check that evaluated and *failed* (`success=False`). The two are
50	    distinct result statuses (#122): an errored check maps to ``error`` (no
51	    severity, no metric), a failed one to a severity tier. A single errored check
52	    never fails its siblings — they still evaluate and persist.
53	    """
54	
55	    expectation_type: str
56	    success: bool
57	    observed_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
58	    expected_value: dict[str, Any] | None = None
59	    sample_failures: dict[str, Any] | None = None
60	    errored: bool = False
61	    error_message: str | None = None
62	    # The badness scalar a *monitor* (freshness/volume, ADR 0012) computed directly
63	    # — age-hours, % volume deviation. `severity.extract_metric` prefers this when
64	    # set, so monitor kinds band the same way (higher = worse, ADR 0016) without
65	    # abusing the GX unexpected-% sample shape. None for GX expectations, whose
66	    # metric is parsed from the sample.
67	    metric_value: float | None = None
68	
69	
70	@dataclass(frozen=True)
71	class MonitorSpec:
72	    """One monitor to evaluate (freshness/volume, ADR 0012), sourced from a `checks`
73	    row whose ``kind`` is a monitor kind. ``config`` is the check's JSONB config
74	    (e.g. ``{"column": "loaded_at"}`` / ``{"min_rows": 1000, "max_rows": 5000}``).
75	
76	    A monitor isn't a GX expectation — it runs a scalar SQL aggregate — so it has its
77	    own spec/runner path distinct from `CheckSpec`/`CheckRunner`.
78	    """
79	
80	    kind: str
81	    config: dict[str, Any]
82	
83

85	class SuiteOutcome:
86	    """Aggregate result of running a list of checks against one table."""
87	
88	    success: bool
89	    checks: list[CheckOutcome]

93	class CheckRunner(Protocol):
94	    """Executes a set of checks against a single table and returns outcomes."""
95	
96	    def run_checks(
97	        self,
98	        *,
99	        table: str,
100	        schema: str | None,
101	        checks: list[CheckSpec],
102	        index_columns: list[str] | None = None,
103	    ) -> SuiteOutcome: ...
104	
105	
106	@runtime_checkable
107	class MonitorRunner(Protocol):
108	    """A datasource runner that can also evaluate **monitor** kinds (freshness/
109	    volume) by running scalar SQL aggregates against the table — the SQL datasources
110	    (Snowflake, Unity Catalog) in v1. Flat-file runners don't implement this, so the
111	    run path can gate monitor checks to SQL datasources via an ``isinstance`` check.
112	
113	    One ``CheckOutcome`` per ``MonitorSpec``, in order. A monitor that can't be
114	    evaluated (bad column, type mismatch) yields an ``errored`` outcome rather than
115	    failing its siblings — mirroring `CheckRunner` semantics.
116	    """
117	
118	    def run_monitors(
119	        self,
120	        *,
121	        table: str,
122	        schema: str | None,
123	        monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
124	    ) -> list[CheckOutcome]: ...
125	
126	
127	@runtime_checkable
128	class ConnectionAdapter(Protocol):
129	    """Per-datasource-type connection behaviour: config validation + live test.
130	
131	    The two things that vary across connection types (Snowflake now; ADF, ADLS,
132	    S3, Unity Catalog next) behind one interface, so connection-CRUD service code
133	    dispatches by ``connection.type`` and never branches on it. Each adapter owns
134	    its own pydantic config model; both methods take the raw config dict so the
135	    adapter is the single source of truth for that type's shape.
136	
137	    `validate_config` parses + validates a stored/incoming config (raising
138	    pydantic ``ValidationError`` on bad input) and returns the normalised model.
139	    `test` resolves connectivity against the live datasource using the config +
140	    its secret, raising on failure. Adapters never touch the DB or SecretStore —
141	    the caller resolves the secret and hands it in.
142	
143	    Some types need MORE than one credential (an Iceberg SQL catalog needs the
144	    storage key *and* the catalog DB password). Rather than smuggle the second one
145	    into non-secret `config` — the #754/#826 bug — the caller resolves every extra
146	    secret named in config and passes them as keyword arguments. Adapters that need
147	    none simply ignore them (`**_`), so the seam's "caller resolves secrets"
148	    invariant holds for one credential or five.
149	    """
150	
151	    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> BaseModel: ...
152	
153	    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **extra_secrets: Any) -> None: ...

result.output:
Found by the 2026-07-09 architecture review (findings register issue links back here). Filed into W4 deliberately: this refactor should land **before or with** schema_drift (#592) so the new kind is an additive registration, not a fourth copy of the if-chains.

## Problem

The monitor-kind seam (ADR 0012) is extensible in the *schema* but not in the *code*. Adding a kind today touches at least three sites:

1. `run_service._run_outcomes` (`backend/app/services/run_service.py:101-156`) — hardcoded two-way partition (`expectation` vs `MONITOR_KINDS`), `NotImplementedError` for the rest.
2. `check_service._V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS` (`backend/app/services/check_service.py:55`) — authoring-side allowlist.
3. `backend/app/datasources/monitors.py` — **three parallel `if kind == FRESHNESS / VOLUME / else raise` chains** repeated across `build_monitor_sql`, `validate_monitor_config`, and `monitor_outcome` (~lines 104-109, 171-176, 192-231).

Fine at 2 kinds; at 4 (schema_drift #592, anomaly #593) the parallel chains become a drift/bug surface — a kind added to one function but not another fails at runtime, not import time.

## Proposed direction

A per-kind strategy object (e.g. `MonitorKindSpec`: `validate_config`, `build_sql` / scalar-source hook, `outcome`) in a registry keyed by kind — mirroring how `_ADAPTERS`/`_RUNNER_BUILDERS` already work in `datasources/registry.py`. `monitors.py`'s three chains collapse into lookups; `_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS` derives from the registry. Keep the `expectation`-vs-monitor split in `_run_outcomes` (it dispatches to a different runner Protocol) but make the monitor side registry-driven.

Note the Iceberg runner variation: it feeds `run_monitor_specs` from native scan scalars instead of SQL aggregates — the strategy interface must keep that scalar-source seam open (it is today's one intended point of variation).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Adding a monitor kind = one registry entry + one strategy class (no edits to `monitors.py` chains or `_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS`)
- [ ] Reserved-kind rejection behavior unchanged (graceful 4xx at authoring, no runtime surprises)
- [ ] Existing freshness/volume behavior covered by the current test suite, green unchanged
- [ ] #592 (schema_drift) implemented on top of the new seam

199	def _client_ip(request: Request, trusted_hops: int) -> str:
200	    """The client IP for per-IP buckets.
201	
202	    X-Forwarded-For is a comma-separated chain — each trusted proxy appends the
203	    peer it saw. Exactly `trusted_hops` proxies are trusted to append (config
204	    `RATE_LIMIT_XFF_TRUSTED_HOPS`), so the real client is the entry
205	    `trusted_hops` from the right (`entries[len - hops]`). Picking a fixed depth
206	    from the right is what survives a multi-proxy chain: a single-proxy compose
207	    setup uses hops=1 (rightmost), while the ACA public-envoy→nginx→internal-
208	    envoy chain uses hops=3.
209	
210	    If the chain is SHORTER than `trusted_hops`, the request did not traverse the
211	    expected proxy stack, so the whole XFF header is untrustworthy → fall back to
212	    the socket peer (never `entries[0]`, the most spoofable position). Whitespace
213	    is tolerated and an IPv4 `:port` suffix stripped; a garbage candidate or a
214	    missing peer falls back to `"unknown"`.
215	    """
216	    xff = request.headers.get("x-forwarded-for")
217	    if xff and trusted_hops >= 1:
218	        entries = [e.strip() for e in xff.split(",")]
219	        if len(entries) >= trusted_hops:
220	            candidate = _strip_ipv4_port(entries[len(entries) - trusted_hops])
221	            if candidate and _is_ip(candidate):
222	                return candidate
223	    client = request.client
224	    if client is not None and client.host:
225	        return client.host
226	    return "unknown"

def _bucket_ip(ip: str, settings: Settings) -> str:
    """The per-IP bucket key component: `ip` folded onto its address prefix (#789).

    Keying per full /32 dilutes the cap against a rotating NAT/proxy pool — the
    pool spreads a burst across many sibling addresses so no single bucket ever
    fills (observed live: 200 requests over 11 distinct IPs in one /24, none near
    the cap). Folding onto a configurable prefix (IPv4 `rate_limit_ipv4_prefix`,
    default /24; IPv6 `rate_limit_ipv6_prefix`, default /64 — one subscriber's
    standard allocation) makes the whole pool share one bucket. The prefix length
    rides in the key, so retuning the mask starts fresh buckets rather than
    cross-counting. A non-address (`"unknown"`) passes through unchanged.
    """
    try:
        addr = ipaddress.ip_address(ip)
    except ValueError:
        return ip
    prefix = (
        settings.rate_limit_ipv4_prefix
        if isinstance(addr, ipaddress.IPv4Address)
        else settings.rate_limit_ipv6_prefix
    )
    network = ipaddress.ip_network((addr, prefix), strict=False)
    return f"{network.network_address}/{prefix}"


def _resolve_policy(
    path: str, bearer: str | None, ip: str, settings: Settings
) -> tuple[str, int, str]:

input.old_string: ### Threat model — the client-IP rule
input.new_string:
### Threat model — per-IP means per address prefix (#789)

Every per-IP bucket (unauth, webhook, and both `ipall` ceilings) keys on an
address **prefix** — IPv4 `/24`, IPv6 `/64` by default
(`RATE_LIMIT_IPV4_PREFIX` / `RATE_LIMIT_IPV6_PREFIX`) — not the full address.
Keying per `/32` dilutes the cap against a **rotating NAT/proxy pool**: the
post-#725 deploy verification observed a 200-request burst from one machine land
on 11 distinct source IPs inside a single `2.57.171.0/24` pool (13–26 requests
each, none near the 120 cap). The same mechanics serve a deliberately
distributed low-rate attacker. Folding onto the allocation prefix makes the pool
share one bucket; `/64` is the standard single-subscriber IPv6 allocation, where
per-address keying would be trivially dilutable (one host holds 2^64 addresses).
Trade-offs, accepted: a prefix that legitimately hosts many independent clients
(CGNAT, corporate egress) shares the cap — the masks are tunable per deployment,
and `/32` / `/128` disable grouping; benign CGNAT users are unaffected in the
default posture (they previously shared a single egress /32 anyway). The prefix
length rides in the key (`ip:2.57.171.0/24`), so retuning starts fresh buckets
instead of cross-counting, and the token-bucket class is IP-independent and
untouched.

### Threat model — the client-IP rule

input.content:
"""The shared SQL-identifier allowlist (#428).

SQL identifiers have no bind-parameter slot, so every path that interpolates a
user-supplied table/schema/column into a query string (monitor config, the
profiler's query builders) must validate against this one decision first —
widening it (e.g. quoted/unicode identifiers) then happens everywhere at once.
Callers keep their own error types; the shared piece is the decision, not the
failure shape.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import re
from typing import Final

# The Snowflake/Databricks unquoted-identifier set: letter/underscore lead, then
# word chars or `$`. Private — go through `is_sql_identifier`, whose isinstance
# guard keeps non-string config values (JSON ints/lists/None) a clean rejection
# instead of a re.match TypeError.
_SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE: Final = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*")


def is_sql_identifier(name: object) -> bool:
    """True when ``name`` is a plain SQL identifier safe to interpolate.

    Anything else (spaces, quotes, dots, trailing newline, non-strings) is
    refused — it can't be made injection-safe by quoting alone at the call sites
    that use this. ``fullmatch`` so a ``$``-anchored trailing ``\\n`` can't slip
    through (``re.match`` + ``$`` accepts one).
    """
    return isinstance(name, str) and bool(_SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE.fullmatch(name))

1	"""Shared SQL-datasource primitives (#428).

3	The single source of truth for two things every SQL-speaking path needs and
4	previously copied per-module:
5	
6	* :data:`SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE` / :func:`is_sql_identifier` — the plain-identifier
7	  allowlist (the Snowflake/Databricks unquoted-identifier set: letter/underscore
8	  lead, then word chars or ``$``). SQL identifiers have no bind-parameter slot,
9	  so every path that interpolates a user-supplied table/schema/column into a
10	  query string (monitor config, the profiler's query builders) must validate
11	  against this exact set first. One definition means widening it (e.g. quoted /
12	  unicode identifiers) happens everywhere at once, never in one copy silently.
13	  Callers keep their own error types (a monitor raises ``MonitorConfigError``,
14	  the profiler a 422 ``ProfileIdentifierInvalidError``) — the shared piece is
15	  the *decision*, not the failure shape.
16	
17	* :func:`run_monitors_over_engine` — the one engine → connection → scalar loop
18	  the SQL runners (Snowflake / Unity Catalog) execute monitor checks through.
19	  The runners differ only in how they build their URL/engine; the lifecycle
20	  (open one connection, feed ``evaluate_monitors`` a scalar fetcher, let a
21	  connection failure propagate to fail the run) is identical and lives here.
22	  The caller owns the engine (build + dispose) — that seam is what lets #427
23	  later thread a per-run shared engine through without touching this helper.
24	"""
25	
26	from __future__ import annotations
27	
28	import re
29	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Final
30	
31	if TYPE_CHECKING:
32	    from sqlalchemy.engine import Engine
33	
34	    from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckOutcome, MonitorSpec
35

43	    made injection-safe by quoting alone at the call sites that use this.
44	    """
45	    return isinstance(name, str) and bool(SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE.match(name))
46	
47	
48	def run_monitors_over_engine(
49	    engine: Engine,
50	    *,
51	    table: str,
52	    schema: str | None,
53	    catalog: str | None,
54	    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
55	) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
56	    """Run monitor checks over ONE connection from ``engine``, one outcome each.
57	
58	    Opens a single connection and sources every monitor's scalar from it via
59	    ``evaluate_monitors`` (a bad monitor errors only itself; a connection-level
60	    failure propagates and fails the whole run — the open happens before the
61	    per-monitor loop). The engine's lifecycle belongs to the caller.
62	    """
63	    # Lazy imports: sqlalchemy is heavy (runner-module convention), and monitors.py
64	    # imports this module for the identifier allowlist — importing it back at
65	    # module scope would be a cycle.
66	    from sqlalchemy import text
67	
68	    from backend.app.datasources.monitors import evaluate_monitors
69	
70	    with engine.connect() as conn:
71	        return evaluate_monitors(
72	            lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
73	            table=table,
74	            schema=schema,
75	            catalog=catalog,
76	            monitors=monitors,
77	        )

import re
from typing import Final

# The Snowflake/Databricks unquoted-identifier set: letter/underscore lead, then
# word chars or `$`. Private — go through `is_sql_identifier`, whose isinstance
# guard keeps a non-string config value (JSON ints/lists/None) a clean rejection
# instead of a re TypeError at the match call.
_SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE: Final = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_$]*")


def is_sql_identifier(name: object) -> bool:
    """True when ``name`` is a plain SQL identifier safe to interpolate.

    Anything else (spaces, quotes, dots, a trailing newline, non-strings) is
    refused — it can't be made injection-safe by quoting alone at the call sites
    that use this. ``fullmatch``, not ``match`` + ``$``: the ``$`` anchor accepts
    one trailing ``\\n``, which has no business in an identifier.
    """
    return isinstance(name, str) and bool(_SQL_IDENTIFIER_RE.fullmatch(name))

"""Monitor kinds — freshness & volume (ADR 0012), the non-GX computed monitors.

A monitor isn't a GX expectation: it runs a single **scalar SQL aggregate** against
the target table and turns the result into a badness ``metric_value`` that the
severity layer bands (higher = worse, ADR 0016), exactly like a GX check's
unexpected-%. This module is the pure, datasource-agnostic core:

* :func:`build_monitor_sql` — the aggregate query a SQL runner executes;
* :func:`monitor_outcome` — scalar result + check config → ``CheckOutcome``.

The per-datasource *execution* (open a connection, run the SQL, fetch the scalar)
lives in the SQL runners; this module never touches a connection, so it is fully
unit-tested. v1 monitors are SQL-datasource only (Snowflake / Unity Catalog).

Semantics (locked):
* **freshness** — config ``{"column": <timestamp col>}``; metric = **age in hours**
  of ``MAX(column)`` vs now (higher = staler = worse). Banded by the check's
  warn/fail/critical thresholds (e.g. warn 24h, fail 48h).
* **volume** — config ``{"min_rows": N, "max_rows": M}``; metric = **% deviation**
  of ``COUNT(*)`` *outside* ``[N, M]`` (either direction; 0 when in range). Banded
  by the thresholds, so a drop *or* a spike past tolerance escalates.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

from collections.abc import Callable
from datetime import UTC, date, datetime, time
from typing import Any

from backend.app.datasources.base import CheckOutcome, MonitorSpec
from backend.app.datasources.sql import is_sql_identifier

FRESHNESS = "freshness"
VOLUME = "volume"
MONITOR_KINDS = (FRESHNESS, VOLUME)
...
                    success=False,
                    errored=True,
                    error_message=str(exc),
                )
            )
    return outcomes


def evaluate_monitors(
    fetch_scalar: Callable[[str], Any],
    *,
    table: str,
    schema: str | None,
    catalog: str | None,
    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
    """Run a list of monitors over an already-open connection via `run_monitor_specs`,
    with the scalar sourced from a SQL aggregate. ``fetch_scalar`` runs a SQL string
    and returns its scalar — the runner closes over its connection, so this stays
    DB-free and unit-testable. Connection *establishment* failure is the runner's
    concern (it opens the connection before calling this)."""
    now = datetime.now(UTC)

    def scalar_for(spec: MonitorSpec) -> Any:
        sql = build_monitor_sql(
            spec.kind, table=table, schema=schema, catalog=catalog, config=spec.config
        )
        return fetch_scalar(sql)

    return run_monitor_specs(scalar_for, monitors=monitors, now=now)

def run_monitors_over_engine(
    engine: Any,
    *,
    table: str,
    schema: str | None,
    catalog: str | None,
    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
) -> list[CheckOutcome]:

    The execution edge the SQL runners (Snowflake / Unity Catalog) share (#428):
    opens a single connection and sources every monitor's scalar from it via
    `evaluate_monitors` (a bad monitor errors only itself; a connection-level
    failure propagates and fails the whole run — the open happens before the
    per-monitor loop). The engine's lifecycle (build + dispose) belongs to the
    caller — the seam #427 threads a per-run shared engine through.
    """

    with engine.connect() as conn:
        return evaluate_monitors(
            lambda sql: conn.execute(text(sql)).scalar(),
            table=table,
            schema=schema,
            catalog=catalog,
            monitors=monitors,
        )
'''

"""Tests for the shared SQL-identifier allowlist (#428) and the deduped
monitor-over-engine execution loop it enabled (now in `monitors.py`)."""

from __future__ import annotations

import pytest
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, event, text

from backend.app.datasources import monitors as monitors_module
from backend.app.datasources.base import MonitorSpec
from backend.app.datasources.monitors import run_monitors_over_engine
from backend.app.datasources.sql import is_sql_identifier
from backend.app.services import profile_service

# ───────────────────────── identifier allowlist ─────────────────────────


@pytest.mark.parametrize("name", ["orders", "_private", "COL$1", "a1_b2", "T"])
def test_valid_identifiers_pass(name: str) -> None:
    assert is_sql_identifier(name)


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "name",
    [
        "",
        "1abc",
        "a b",
        'a"b',
        "a;drop table t",
        "a.b",
        "col-name",
        "col\n",  # fullmatch: the `$`-anchor loophole (one trailing \n) is closed
        None,
        42,
        ["orders"],
    ],
)
def test_invalid_identifiers_and_non_strings_fail(name: object) -> None:
    assert not is_sql_identifier(name)


def test_monitors_ident_routes_through_shared_allowlist(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    # Pin the ROUTING, not just behavioral agreement (#428's whole point): with
    # the shared predicate forced to False, the consumer must reject a name it
    # would otherwise accept — proving it has no private regex copy.
    from backend.app.datasources.monitors import MonitorConfigError, _ident

    assert _ident("fine_col", what="column") == "fine_col"
    monkeypatch.setattr(monitors_module, "is_sql_identifier", lambda name: False)
    with pytest.raises(MonitorConfigError):
        _ident("fine_col", what="column")


def test_profiler_validate_identifier_routes_through_shared_allowlist(
    monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch,
) -> None:
    from backend.app.services.profile_service import (
        ProfileIdentifierInvalidError,
        validate_identifier,
    )

    assert validate_identifier("fine_col") == "fine_col"
    monkeypatch.setattr(profile_service, "is_sql_identifier", lambda name: False)
    with pytest.raises(ProfileIdentifierInvalidError):
        validate_identifier("fine_col")


# ───────────────────────── run_monitors_over_engine ─────────────────────────


@pytest.fixture
def engine():  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A file-backed DB (not sqlite:// in-memory, whose SingletonThreadPool hands
    # every connect() the same DBAPI connection and would mask an extra open).
    eng = None

    def _build(tmp_path):  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
        nonlocal eng
        eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/monitors.sqlite")
        with eng.begin() as conn:
            conn.execute(text("CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER)"))
            conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO orders (id) VALUES (1), (2), (3)"))
        return eng

    yield _build
    if eng is not None:
        eng.dispose()


def test_monitors_share_exactly_one_connection(tmp_path, engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # The helper's contract (and #427's cost story): ONE DBAPI connect per call,
    # however many monitors run. Counted via the pool's connect event — outcomes
    # alone can't detect a regression to connect-per-monitor.
    eng = engine(tmp_path)
    connects: list[object] = []
    event.listen(eng, "connect", lambda dbapi_conn, rec: connects.append(dbapi_conn))
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        eng,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[
            MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10}),
            MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 5, "max_rows": 10}),
        ],
    )
    assert len(connects) == 1
    assert len(outcomes) == 2
    assert outcomes[0].success
    assert outcomes[0].metric_value == 0.0
    assert outcomes[0].observed_value == {"row_count": 3, "deviation_pct": 0.0}
    assert not outcomes[1].success  # 3 rows < floor 5 → deviation


def test_bad_monitor_errors_only_itself(tmp_path, engine) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # First monitor queries a nonexistent column (SQL error at fetch time); the
    # sibling volume monitor on the same connection must still produce a result.
    eng = engine(tmp_path)
    outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
        eng,
        table="orders",
        schema=None,
        catalog=None,
        monitors=[
            MonitorSpec(kind="freshness", config={"column": "no_such_col"}),
            MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10}),
        ],
    )
    assert [o.errored for o in outcomes] == [True, False]
    assert outcomes[1].success


def test_connection_failure_propagates(tmp_path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A datasource-establishment failure must fail the whole run (raise), never
    # degrade into per-monitor errors — the connect happens before the loop.
    eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/no_such_dir/db.sqlite")
    with pytest.raises(Exception, match="unable to open database file"):
        run_monitors_over_engine(
            eng,
            table="orders",
            schema=None,
            catalog=None,
            monitors=[MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 0, "max_rows": 1})],
        )

def _seeded_engine(tmp_path):  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A file-backed DB (not sqlite:// in-memory, whose SingletonThreadPool hands
    # every connect() the same DBAPI connection and would mask an extra open).
    eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/monitors.sqlite")
    with eng.begin() as conn:
        conn.execute(text("CREATE TABLE orders (id INTEGER)"))
        conn.execute(text("INSERT INTO orders (id) VALUES (1), (2), (3)"))
    eng.dispose()  # drop the seeding connection so the test counts from zero
    return eng


def test_monitors_share_exactly_one_connection(tmp_path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # The helper's contract (and #427's cost story): ONE DBAPI connect per call,
    # however many monitors run. Counted via the pool's connect event — outcomes
    # alone can't detect a regression to connect-per-monitor.
    eng = _seeded_engine(tmp_path)
    connects: list[object] = []
    event.listen(eng, "connect", lambda dbapi_conn, rec: connects.append(dbapi_conn))
    try:
        outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
            eng,
            table="orders",
            schema=None,
            catalog=None,
            monitors=[
                MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 1, "max_rows": 10}),
                MonitorSpec(kind="volume", config={"min_rows": 5, "max_rows": 10}),
            ],
        )
    finally:
        eng.dispose()
    assert len(connects) == 1
    assert len(outcomes) == 2

    assert not outcomes[1].success  # 3 rows < floor 5 → volume deviation

    # First monitor queries a nonexistent column (SQL error at fetch time); the

    eng = _seeded_engine(tmp_path)
    try:
        outcomes = run_monitors_over_engine(
            eng,
            table="orders",
            schema=None,
            catalog=None,
            monitors=[
                MonitorSpec(kind="freshness", config={"column": "no_such_col"}),

            ],
        )
    finally:
        eng.dispose()
    assert [o.errored for o in outcomes] == [True, False]
    assert outcomes[1].success


def test_connection_failure_propagates(tmp_path) -> None:  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]
    # A datasource-establishment failure must fail the whole run (raise), never
    # degrade into per-monitor errors — the connect happens before the loop.
    eng = create_engine(f"sqlite:///{tmp_path}/no_such_dir/db.sqlite")
    with pytest.raises(Exception, match="unable to open database file"):

            eng,

    if client is not None and client.host:

def _bucket_ip(ip: str, settings: Settings) -> str:
    """The per-IP bucket key component: `ip` folded onto its address prefix (#789).

    Keying per full /32 dilutes the cap against a rotating NAT/proxy pool — the
    pool spreads a burst across many sibling addresses so no single bucket ever
    fills (observed live: 200 requests over 11 distinct IPs in one /24, none near
    the cap). Folding onto a configurable prefix (IPv4 `rate_limit_ipv4_prefix`,
    default /24; IPv6 `rate_limit_ipv6_prefix`, default /64 — one subscriber's
    standard allocation) makes the whole pool share one bucket. The prefix length
    rides in the key, so retuning the mask starts fresh buckets rather than
    cross-counting. A non-address (`"unknown"`) passes through unchanged.
    """
    try:
        addr = ipaddress.ip_address(ip)
    except ValueError:
        return ip
    prefix = (
        settings.rate_limit_ipv4_prefix
        if isinstance(addr, ipaddress.IPv4Address)
        else settings.rate_limit_ipv6_prefix
    )
    network = ipaddress.ip_network((addr, prefix), strict=False)
    return f"{network.network_address}/{prefix}"


def _resolve_policy(

input.new_string:
rides in the key, so retuning the mask starts fresh buckets rather than
    cross-counting. A non-address (`"unknown"`) passes through unchanged.

    An IPv4-mapped IPv6 address (`::ffff:a.b.c.d` — what a dual-stack `[::]`
    listener's socket peer or a proxy-written XFF entry carries) is unwrapped and
    folded as IPv4: every mapped address lives in `::ffff:0:0/96`, so folding it
    at /64 would collapse the ENTIRE IPv4 internet into one `::/64` bucket — one
    abusive client would 429 all unauthenticated IPv4 traffic — and the same
    client would key differently as `a.b.c.d` vs `::ffff:a.b.c.d`.
    """
    try:
        addr = ipaddress.ip_address(ip)
    except ValueError:
        return ip
    if isinstance(addr, ipaddress.IPv6Address) and addr.ipv4_mapped is not None:
        addr = addr.ipv4_mapped
    prefix = (
        settings.rate_limit_ipv4_prefix
        if addr.version == 4
        else settings.rate_limit_ipv6_prefix
    )
    return str(ipaddress.ip_network((addr, prefix), strict=False))

def test_bucket_ip_ipv4_mapped_ipv6_unwraps_to_ipv4() -> None:
    # A dual-stack `[::]` listener's socket peer (and some proxy XFF chains)
    # carries `::ffff:a.b.c.d`. Folding that as IPv6 would put the ENTIRE IPv4
    # internet into one ::/64 bucket; it must unwrap and fold as IPv4, keying
    # identically to its dotted-quad twin.
    s = _settings()
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("::ffff:9.9.9.1", s) == "9.9.9.0/24"
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("::ffff:9.9.9.1", s) == rate_limit._bucket_ip("9.9.9.2", s)
    assert rate_limit._bucket_ip("::ffff:203.0.113.7", s) != rate_limit._bucket_ip(
        "::ffff:9.9.9.1", s
    )

def test_ipv4_mapped_ipv6_shares_the_dotted_quad_bucket(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
    # A dual-stack chain can present the same client as `9.9.9.x` on one request
    # and `::ffff:9.9.9.x` on another — both must land in the ONE /24 bucket
    # (and never in a shared ::/64 that would collapse all IPv4 clients).
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.9.1"}).status_code != 429
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "::ffff:9.9.9.2"}).status_code != 429
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.9.3"}).status_code != 429
    # 4th in the same /24 (mapped form) → 429; an unrelated IPv4 /24 stays fresh.
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "::ffff:9.9.9.4"}).status_code == 429
    assert limiter.get(PROBE, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "7.7.7.7"}).status_code != 429


def test_bearer_ipall_ceiling_keys_on_prefix(ip_ceiling_limiter: TestClient) -> None:
    # The combined #725+#789 attack: rotate a fresh bearer AND sibling pool IPs.
    # Each request mints a fresh tok bucket, but the ipall ceiling (4) must
    # accumulate across the /24 — pinning that the ceiling key uses the prefix
    # bucket, not the raw /32.
    for i in range(4):
        h = {"Authorization": f"Bearer rot-{i}", "X-Forwarded-For": f"9.9.9.{10 + i}"}
        assert ip_ceiling_limiter.get(PROBE, headers=h).status_code != 429
    resp = ip_ceiling_limiter.get(
        PROBE, headers={"Authorization": "Bearer rot-x", "X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.9.200"}
    )
    assert resp.status_code == 429
    assert resp.headers["X-RateLimit-Limit"] == "4"  # the ipall ceiling, reported


def test_webhook_bucket_keys_on_prefix(limiter: TestClient) -> None:
    # The per-provider webhook bucket must also accumulate across sibling /32s.
    adf = "/api/v1/orchestration/events/adf"
    for i in range(2):  # WEBHOOK limit = 2
        assert limiter.post(adf, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": f"9.9.9.{20 + i}"}).status_code != 429
    assert limiter.post(adf, headers={"X-Forwarded-For": "9.9.9.99"}).status_code == 429

content.text: Now the three doc corrections:

input.command: sed -n '30,40p' docs/rest-api.md; grep -n "unaffected in the default posture\|per client-IP\|rl:webhook:\*:ip:\*" docs/adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md | head

result.output:
- **Versioning:** all endpoints are under `/api/v1`.
- **Errors:** a JSON envelope — `{"error": {"code": "...", "message": "...", "detail": {...}}}` —
  with a conventional HTTP status (`401` auth, `403` forbidden, `404` not found / hidden,
  `422` validation, `409` conflict, `429` rate limited, `502` datasource unreachable).
- **Rate limiting (ADR 0035):** every surface is throttled per minute — authenticated
  requests per API key, unauthenticated per client-IP. Over the limit returns `429` with
  `code: "rate_limited"`, `detail.retry_after_seconds` (1–60), and a matching `Retry-After`
  header (plus `X-RateLimit-Limit` / `X-RateLimit-Remaining`). Back off for that many seconds.
- **IDs** are UUIDs. Timestamps are ISO-8601 UTC.

56:  | `webhook` | `/api/v1/orchestration/events/*` | per provider **+** client-IP (**even with a bearer** — machine path), **plus** a per-IP `ipall` ceiling. The known provider segment (adf/airflow/dbt, from the shared `ORCHESTRATION_PROVIDERS` vocabulary) is folded into the key so one noisy orchestrator can't crowd out another's callbacks from the same egress IP (#785); an *unknown* segment shares one bare-IP bucket, so a path scanner can't mint fresh buckets by rotating the segment; and the `rl:webhook:ipall:{ip}` ceiling (`RATE_LIMIT_WEBHOOK_IP_PER_MINUTE`) bounds the **aggregate** one IP can spend across provider buckets — without it, per-provider folding would quietly multiply the per-IP webhook budget by (providers + 1). Ops note: provider-folded keys match `rl:webhook:*:ip:*` in a Redis SCAN, not the pre-#785 `rl:webhook:ip:*` | 120 (per provider bucket) / 240 (`ipall`) |
58:  | `unauth` | everything else | per client-IP | 120 |

# rest-api.md
src = open('docs/rest-api.md').read()
old = "requests per API key, unauthenticated per client-IP."
new = "requests per API key, unauthenticated per client-IP **prefix** (IPv4 /24, IPv6 /64 by default — machines on one allocation share a budget; ADR 0035 #789)."
assert old in src
open('docs/rest-api.md','w').write(src.replace(old, new, 1))

# ADR 0035
src = open('docs/adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md').read()
old = "Ops note: provider-folded keys match `rl:webhook:*:ip:*` in a Redis SCAN, not the pre-#785 `rl:webhook:ip:*` | 120 (per provider bucket) / 240 (`ipall`) |"
new = "Ops note: provider-folded keys match `rl:webhook:*:ip:*` in a Redis SCAN, not the pre-#785 `rl:webhook:ip:*` — and since #789 the ip component is the **folded network** (`ip:2.57.171.0/24`), so an exact-address lookup (`MATCH *ip:203.0.113.7*`) returns nothing even at /32 (`ip:203.0.113.7/32`); SCAN with the folded form | 120 (per provider bucket) / 240 (`ipall`) |"
assert old in src
src = src.replace(old, new, 1)
old = "  | `unauth` | everything else | per client-IP | 120 |"
new = "  | `unauth` | everything else | per client-IP | 120 |\n\n  \"Per client-IP\" throughout this table means per client-IP **prefix** since #789 — see the threat-model section below.\n"
assert old in src
src = src.replace(old, new, 1)
old = """and `/32` / `/128` disable grouping; benign CGNAT users are unaffected in the
default posture (they previously shared a single egress /32 anyway). The prefix"""
new = """and `/32` / `/128` disable grouping. NB: the "unaffected" case is only
single-egress-IP NAT (those users already shared one /32 bucket); **pool-based
CGNAT** — a carrier NATing subscribers across a /24-or-wider pool — previously
spread across many independent buckets and now shares one, so a CGNAT-heavy user
base may genuinely need a longer mask (or /32). The prefix"""
assert old in src
src = src.replace(old, new, 1)
open('docs/adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md','w').write(src)
print("ok")
EOF

git add -A && git commit -m "fixup: #874 review — unwrap IPv4-mapped IPv6, CGNAT wording, prefix-wiring pins, doc sync

- ::ffff:a.b.c.d (dual-stack socket peer / proxy XFF form) took the IPv6
  branch, and every mapped address folds to ::/64 — ALL IPv4 clients in one
  bucket: one abusive client would 429 the whole unauthenticated IPv4
  surface, and the same client keyed differently as a.b.c.d vs its mapped
  twin. Now unwrapped via addr.ipv4_mapped and folded as IPv4; pinned in
  unit + middleware tests.
- ADR 0035's 'benign CGNAT users unaffected' claim was false for POOL-based
  CGNAT (the very mechanic the section documents); reworded to scope the
  unaffected case to single-egress-IP NAT.
- Test pins added for the 'every per-IP key site uses the prefix' promise:
  rotated-bearer + sibling-/32 attack trips the ipall ceiling; webhook
  provider bucket accumulates across sibling /32s. Settings bounds test now
  pins the ge/le constraint (ValidationError) instead of any error naming
  the field, incl. an IPv6 case.
- docs/rest-api.md per-client-IP wording, ADR class-table prefix pointer +
  exact-address SCAN note (folded-network form); _bucket_ip simplified to
  str(ip_network(...)) + addr.version.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push

post backend/app/core/rate_limit.py 262 "**[review — correctness/security, CONFIRMED (both reviewers), fixed in 7f4b31da]** IPv4-mapped IPv6 (\`::ffff:a.b.c.d\` — what a dual-stack \`[::]\` listener's socket peer or a proxy-written XFF entry carries) took the IPv6 branch, and every mapped address lives in \`::ffff:0:0/96\`, so **all of them folded to \`::/64\`** — the entire IPv4 internet in one bucket. One abusive client would have 429'd all unauthenticated IPv4 traffic (incl. the ADR-0032 OTP surface this limiter is the named prereq for), and the same client keyed differently as \`9.9.9.9\` vs \`::ffff:9.9.9.9\`. Fixed by unwrapping \`addr.ipv4_mapped\` before the version branch; pinned in unit + middleware tests."
post docs/adr/0035-request-rate-limiting.md 111 "**[review — docs correctness, CONFIRMED, fixed in 7f4b31da]** The claim 'benign CGNAT users are unaffected in the default posture (they previously shared a single egress /32 anyway)' was false for **pool-based** CGNAT — a carrier NATing subscribers across a /24-or-wider pool previously spread across many independent buckets and now shares one; the sentence also contradicted the paragraph's own opening trade-off and deploy/README's '/32 for CGNAT-heavy' guidance. Reworded to scope the unaffected case to single-egress-IP NAT."
post backend/tests/api/test_rate_limiting.py 356 "**[review — test coverage, CONFIRMED, fixed in 7f4b31da]** The 'every per-IP key site uses the prefix' promise rested on one wiring line with only the unauth class pinned e2e — a refactor moving \`_bucket_ip\` into \`_resolve_policy\` (or re-deriving the raw address for the middleware-built \`ipall\` keys) would have silently reverted the ceilings to per-/32, letting the combined rotate-bearer + rotate-sibling-IP attack mint unlimited buckets. Added: rotated bearers across sibling /32s trip the \`ipall\` ceiling; webhook provider bucket accumulates across sibling /32s; IPv4-mapped form shares the dotted-quad /24 end-to-end."
post backend/tests/core/test_rate_limit.py 158 "**[review — test fidelity, CONFIRMED, fixed in 7f4b31da]** \`pytest.raises(Exception, match='rate_limit_ipv4_prefix')\` also passes on the extra-forbidden error if the field were deleted outright — it couldn't distinguish 'value out of ge/le bounds' from 'env-var contract silently dropped'. Now pins \`ValidationError\` + the constraint message, with an IPv6-bounds case added."
post docs/rest-api.md 36 "**[review — docs, CONFIRMED, fixed in 7f4b31da]** 'unauthenticated per client-IP' was stale after this PR (a second machine on the same office /24 does NOT get an independent budget). Now says per client-IP prefix with the defaults; ADR class table gained the same pointer plus an ops note that exact-address Redis SCAN lookups must use the folded-network form (\`ip:2.57.171.0/24\`) — the #725 lesson about probes that can't see the counting."
post backend/app/core/config.py 152 "**[review — accepted, no change]** A malformed \`RATE_LIMIT_IPV4_PREFIX\` env value fails Settings construction at startup (fail-closed for the whole API) while the limiter itself is fail-open at runtime. Accepted deliberately: this matches every other Settings field's behavior (\`extra=forbid\` posture — a typo'd knob should be caught at deploy time, not silently defaulted mid-incident), and the ge/le bounds make the failure message name the field and constraint."

*,
    config: dict[str, Any],
    secret_ref: str | None,
    secret_store: SecretStore,
) -> SnowflakeCheckRunner:
    """Build a runner from a `Connection` row's config + secret_ref.

    Takes primitives (not the ORM model) to keep the adapter decoupled from the
    DB layer.
    """
    if not secret_ref:
        raise ValueError("Snowflake connection requires secret_ref for the password / private key")
    sf_config = SnowflakeConfig.model_validate(config)
    secret = secret_store.get(secret_ref)
    return SnowflakeCheckRunner(sf_config, secret)


# Snowflake connector timeouts (seconds) for the connectivity test — fail fast
# rather than hanging the request thread on an unreachable account.
_TEST_LOGIN_TIMEOUT = 10
_TEST_NETWORK_TIMEOUT = 10


class SnowflakeConnectionAdapter:
    """`ConnectionAdapter` for Snowflake — config validation + a SELECT 1 test."""

    def validate_config(self, raw: dict[str, Any]) -> SnowflakeConfig:
        return SnowflakeConfig.model_validate(raw)

    def test(self, raw: dict[str, Any], secret: str, **_: Any) -> None:
        """Open a connection and run ``SELECT 1``; raise on any failure.

        Deliberately GX-free — a lightweight connectivity probe, not a suite run.
        """
        from sqlalchemy import create_engine, text

        config = self.validate_config(raw)
        engine = create_engine(
            build_connection_string(config, secret),
            connect_args={
                "login_timeout": _TEST_LOGIN_TIMEOUT,
                "network_timeout": _TEST_NETWORK_TIMEOUT,
                **build_connect_args(config, secret),
            },
        )
        try:
            with engine.connect() as conn:
                conn.execute(text("SELECT 1"))
        finally:
            engine.dispose()

100	    config: UnityCatalogConfig, token: str, *, catalog: str | None = None
101	) -> str:
102	    """SQLAlchemy URL for the Databricks SQL Warehouse (databricks dialect).
103	
104	    The PAT, http_path and `catalog` are URL-encoded. Pinning `catalog` sets the
105	    session default so a 2-level `schema.table` reference resolves to
106	    `catalog.schema.table`; the profiler leaves it unset and qualifies the
107	    namespace in the query instead.
108	    """
109	    url = (
110	        f"REDACTED"
111	        f"?http_path={quote_plus(config.http_path)}"
112	    )
113	    if catalog:
114	        url += f"&catalog={quote_plus(catalog)}"
115	    return url
116

118	class UnityCatalogCheckRunner:
119	    """GX `CheckRunner` for Unity Catalog via the Databricks SQL Warehouse.
120	
121	    The UC run path reads the target table into a pandas DataFrame and validates
122	    that frame with GX — the "GX DataFrame datasource" shape (CLAUDE.md §5), the
123	    same shape Databricks Labs DQX consumes, so v1.1 can swap GX for DQX behind
124	    this same interface without touching the suite/check/result layer.
125	
126	    `table` + `schema` come from `run_checks` (the suite's target); `catalog` is
127	    fixed per run (held here). Reflecting + reading the table is the live seam
128	    (`_read_table`), monkeypatched in tests; GX then runs in-process on the
129	    returned frame, so the validation path itself is fully covered.
130	    """
131	
132	    def __init__(self, *, config: UnityCatalogConfig, token: str, catalog: str) -> None:
133	        self._config = config
134	        self._token = token
135	        self._catalog = catalog
136	
137	    def _read_table(self, *, table: str, schema: str | None) -> Any:
138	        """Reflect + read the whole table into a DataFrame (live seam).
139	
140	        `read_sql_table` reflects through SQLAlchemy (proper dialect quoting), so
141	        the table/schema identifiers are never string-formatted into SQL; the
142	        pinned catalog + `schema` qualify it to `catalog.schema.table`.
143	        """
144	        import pandas as pd
145	        from sqlalchemy import create_engine
146

149	        )
