conda run -n dataq python -m pytest backend/tests -q 2>&1 | tail -1 && git add -A && git commit -m "fixup: #879 review — delete guard on volume too, absent totals degrade, config errors escape

- The severity find: summary total-records nets only DATA-file records
  (verified vs pyiceberg's _update_totals) — on a merge-on-read table with
  live position/equality deletes it over-counts vs the delete-aware
  scan().count(), so trusting it would false-PASS a volume floor. The
  row-level-delete guard now applies to BOTH metadata paths via one shared
  _row_delete_guard.
- The total-*-deletes summary fields are OPTIONAL: an absent field now
  means 'cannot prove no deletes' (degrade), never 'no deletes'. Plus the
  authoritative per-task check: scan tasks carrying delete_files refuse the
  bounds path even when the summary claims clean.
- An unknown freshness column on a real schema now raises the check's own
  MonitorConfigError instead of being relabeled 'metadata unavailable' and
  surviving only because the fallback scan happened to re-raise.

All three pinned in tests (false-PASS scenario uses a 1,000,000-row summary
over a 3-row truth); 45 iceberg tests green.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push && HEAD=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
post() { gh api "repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/879/comments" -f commit_id="$HEAD" -f path="$1" -F line="$2" -f side=RIGHT -f body="$3" --jq .id; }
post backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py 470 "**[review — correctness/severity, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** Summary \`total-records\` nets only data-file records (verified against pyiceberg's \`_update_totals\`) — it never subtracts position/equality deletes, while \`scan().count()\` is delete-aware. On a merge-on-read table (1M total-records, 400k position deletes, floor 700k) the metadata path returned 1M → deviation 0 → **false PASS** where the scan would have fired the alert. The freshness path refused metadata under deletes for exactly this reason; volume now shares the same \`_row_delete_guard\`."
post backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py 447 "**[review — fail-open, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** The delete check treated an ABSENT optional summary field as proof of no deletes (\`continue\`) — a writer that omits \`total-delete-files\` would have unlocked the bounds path over live delete rows (the exact false-green the docstring promises to prevent). Absent now means 'cannot prove' → degrade; and the plan_files loop checks the authoritative per-task \`delete_files\` as a second layer."
post backend/app/datasources/iceberg.py 512 "**[review — error semantics, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** \`find_field\`'s unknown-column ValueError was swallowed by the fast-path's broad except, relabeled 'metadata unavailable', and the outcome was only correct because the fallback scan happened to re-raise on the same column — right by coincidence. A real schema lacking the column now raises the check's own \`MonitorConfigError\` (per-check error, #122), pinned with \`scan_calls == 0\`; genuine metadata unavailability (non-standard table objects) still degrades."

96	) -> str:
97	    """The scalar-aggregate SQL a SQL runner executes for this monitor.

100	    Identifiers are validated (no bind slot for them), so a bad column/table raises
101	    :class:`MonitorConfigError` rather than building an injectable query.

105	    # regex) before interpolation. SQL identifiers can't be bound parameters, so
106	    # validated interpolation is the correct construction — not an injection vector

119	    return max((now - max_timestamp).total_seconds() / 3600.0, 0.0)

141	    """Percent the row count falls **outside** ``[min_rows, max_rows]`` (0 in range).
142	
143	    Below the floor → shortfall vs the floor; above the ceiling → excess vs the
144	    ceiling. Symmetric so a drop and a spike both escalate. Guards a zero bound."""
145	    if row_count < min_rows:
146	        return (min_rows - row_count) / min_rows * 100.0 if min_rows else 100.0
147	    if row_count > max_rows:
148	        return (row_count - max_rows) / max_rows * 100.0 if max_rows else 100.0
149	    return 0.0
150	
151	
152	def _volume_bounds(config: dict[str, Any]) -> tuple[int, int]:
153	    """Validate the ``min_rows``/``max_rows`` range from a volume check's config."""
154	    try:
155	        min_rows = int(config["min_rows"])
156	        max_rows = int(config["max_rows"])
157	    except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc:
158	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume needs integer min_rows/max_rows: {config!r}") from exc
159	    if min_rows < 0 or max_rows < min_rows:
160	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume range must be 0 <= min_rows <= max_rows: {config!r}")
161	    return min_rows, max_rows
162	
163	
164	def validate_monitor_config(kind: str, config: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
165	    """Static (DB-free) validation of a monitor check's ``config`` — the *structural*
166	    checks that don't need a live query: a valid ``column`` identifier (freshness) or
167	    a well-formed ``min_rows``/``max_rows`` range (volume). Raises
168	    :class:`MonitorConfigError` on a bad/missing config or unknown kind.
169	
170	    Shared by the **check-authoring** path (reject a malformed monitor at create/update
171	    time with a 422, not silently at the next run) and implicitly by the run path
172	    (`build_monitor_sql`/`monitor_outcome` re-derive the same checks). This is only the
173	    config-shape gate; threshold policy (e.g. freshness *requires* a threshold) and the
174	    SQL-datasource gate live in the service layer, which owns the Check + connection."""
175	    if kind == FRESHNESS:
176	        _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")
177	    elif kind == VOLUME:
178	        _volume_bounds(config)
179	    else:
180	        raise MonitorConfigError(f"unknown monitor kind: {kind!r}")
181	
182	
183	def monitor_outcome(
184	    kind: str, *, scalar: Any, config: dict[str, Any], now: datetime
185	) -> CheckOutcome:
186	    """Turn a monitor's scalar aggregate result into a ``CheckOutcome``.
187	
188	    ``scalar`` is what ``build_monitor_sql`` selected: the ``MAX(column)`` timestamp
189	    (freshness) or the ``COUNT(*)`` (volume). The returned outcome carries a direct
190	    ``metric_value`` (age-hours / deviation-%) for the severity layer to band, plus
191	    a human ``observed_value``/``expected_value`` (no row data → no sample/PII). A
192	    freshness check on an empty table (``MAX`` is NULL) can't be assessed, so it's an
193	    operational ``error`` (#122), not a silent pass.
194	    """
195	    expectation_type = f"{_EXPECTATION_PREFIX}{kind}"
196	    if kind == FRESHNESS:
197	        column = _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")
198	        if scalar is None:
199	            return CheckOutcome(
200	                expectation_type=expectation_type,
201	                success=False,
202	                errored=True,
203	                error_message=f"no rows: MAX({column}) is NULL, freshness can't be assessed",
204	                expected_value={"monitor": FRESHNESS, "column": column},
205	            )
206	        max_ts = _as_aware_datetime(scalar, column)
207	        age_hours = _freshness_age_hours(max_ts, now)
208	        # NOTE: freshness has no in-config bound (unlike volume's min/max_rows), so
209	        # the binary fallback is unconditionally `success=True` — "stale" is only
210	        # defined by a threshold. A freshness check WITHOUT a fail/critical age
211	        # threshold therefore always resolves `pass` no matter how stale (the metric
212	        # is computed but never banded). The check-create path (the monitor-authoring
213	        # slice) MUST require a freshness threshold so this never ships as silent green.
214	        return CheckOutcome(
215	            expectation_type=expectation_type,
216	            success=True,  # binary fallback when no thresholds; thresholds band the age
217	            metric_value=age_hours,
218	            observed_value={"max_timestamp": max_ts.isoformat(), "age_hours": round(age_hours, 3)},
219	            expected_value={"monitor": FRESHNESS, "column": column},
220	        )
221	    if kind == VOLUME:
222	        min_rows, max_rows = _volume_bounds(config)
223	        try:
224	            row_count = int(scalar)
225	        except (TypeError, ValueError) as exc:
226	            raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume COUNT(*) is not an integer: {scalar!r}") from exc
227	        deviation = _volume_deviation_pct(row_count, min_rows=min_rows, max_rows=max_rows)
228	        return CheckOutcome(
229	            expectation_type=expectation_type,
230	            success=deviation == 0.0,  # in range → pass; thresholds band the deviation
231	            metric_value=deviation,
232	            observed_value={"row_count": row_count, "deviation_pct": round(deviation, 3)},
233	            expected_value={"monitor": VOLUME, "min_rows": min_rows, "max_rows": max_rows},
234	        )
235	    raise MonitorConfigError(f"unknown monitor kind: {kind!r}")
236

238	def run_monitor_specs(
239	    scalar_for: Callable[[MonitorSpec], Any],

241	    monitors: list[MonitorSpec],
242	    now: datetime,
243	) -> list[CheckOutcome]:
244	    """Band a list of monitors given a per-spec scalar source, one ``CheckOutcome``

    ``freshness`` → ``SELECT MAX(<column>) ...``; ``volume`` → ``SELECT COUNT(*) ...``.
    Identifiers are validated (no bind slot for them), so a bad column/table raises

# ───────────────────── per-kind strategies (#726) ─────────────────────
#
# Adding a monitor kind = one strategy entry in MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY below —
# never a third parallel if-chain. `build_sql` receives the already-validated
# qualified target; identifiers can't be bound parameters, so validated
# interpolation is the correct construction (hence the S608 suppressions).


def _validate_freshness(config: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")


def _freshness_sql(target: str, config: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    column = _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")
    return f"SELECT MAX({column}) FROM {target}"  # noqa: S608  # nosec B608


def _freshness_outcome(scalar: Any, config: dict[str, Any], now: datetime) -> CheckOutcome:
    expectation_type = monitor_expectation_type(FRESHNESS)
    column = _ident(config.get("column"), what="freshness column")
    if scalar is None:
        return CheckOutcome(
            expectation_type=expectation_type,
            success=False,
            errored=True,
            error_message=f"no rows: MAX({column}) is NULL, freshness can't be assessed",
            expected_value={"monitor": FRESHNESS, "column": column},
        )
    max_ts = _as_aware_datetime(scalar, column)
    age_hours = _freshness_age_hours(max_ts, now)
    # NOTE: freshness has no in-config bound (unlike volume's min/max_rows), so
    # the binary fallback is unconditionally `success=True` — "stale" is only
    # defined by a threshold. A freshness check WITHOUT a fail/critical age
    # threshold therefore always resolves `pass` no matter how stale (the metric
    # is computed but never banded). The check-create path (the monitor-authoring
    # slice) MUST require a freshness threshold so this never ships as silent green.
    return CheckOutcome(
        expectation_type=expectation_type,
        success=True,  # binary fallback when no thresholds; thresholds band the age
        metric_value=age_hours,
        observed_value={"max_timestamp": max_ts.isoformat(), "age_hours": round(age_hours, 3)},
        expected_value={"monitor": FRESHNESS, "column": column},
    )


def _validate_volume(config: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
    _volume_bounds(config)


def _volume_sql(target: str, config: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    return f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {target}"  # noqa: S608  # nosec B608


def _volume_outcome(scalar: Any, config: dict[str, Any], now: datetime) -> CheckOutcome:
    min_rows, max_rows = _volume_bounds(config)
    try:
        row_count = int(scalar)
    except (TypeError, ValueError) as exc:
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"volume COUNT(*) is not an integer: {scalar!r}") from exc
    deviation = _volume_deviation_pct(row_count, min_rows=min_rows, max_rows=max_rows)
    return CheckOutcome(
        expectation_type=monitor_expectation_type(VOLUME),
        success=deviation == 0.0,  # in range → pass; thresholds band the deviation
        metric_value=deviation,
        observed_value={"row_count": row_count, "deviation_pct": round(deviation, 3)},
        expected_value={"monitor": VOLUME, "min_rows": min_rows, "max_rows": max_rows},
    )


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class MonitorKindStrategy:
    """One monitor kind's behavior behind the #726 registry.

    ``validate_config`` is the DB-free structural gate; ``outcome`` bands the
    scalar; ``build_sql`` renders the scalar-aggregate over an already-validated
    qualified target — ``None`` for kinds with no scalar-SQL form (the stateful
    kinds, #592/#593, evaluate through their own path)."""

    kind: str
    validate_config: Callable[[dict[str, Any]], None]
    outcome: Callable[[Any, dict[str, Any], datetime], CheckOutcome]
    build_sql: Callable[[str, dict[str, Any]], str] | None


MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY: dict[str, MonitorKindStrategy] = {
    FRESHNESS: MonitorKindStrategy(FRESHNESS, _validate_freshness, _freshness_outcome, _freshness_sql),
    VOLUME: MonitorKindStrategy(VOLUME, _validate_volume, _volume_outcome, _volume_sql),
}

# Derived, never hand-maintained: the authoring allowlist (check_service) and the
# run-path partition (run_service) both key off this, so registering a kind above
# is the ONLY step that widens them.
MONITOR_KINDS = tuple(MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY)


def _strategy(kind: str) -> MonitorKindStrategy:
    strategy = MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY.get(kind)
    if strategy is None:
        raise MonitorConfigError(f"unknown monitor kind: {kind!r}")
    return strategy
'''
assert old in src
src = src.replace(old, new, 1)

src = src.replace(
    "from collections.abc import Callable",
    "from collections.abc import Callable\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass",
    1,
)
open('backend/app/datasources/monitors.py','w').write(src)
print("ok")

class MonitorRunner(Protocol):

    run path can gate monitor checks to SQL datasources via an ``isinstance`` check.

new = '''@runtime_checkable
class MonitorRunner(Protocol):
    """A datasource runner that can also evaluate **monitor** kinds by running
    scalar aggregates against the table — Snowflake / Unity Catalog (SQL) and
    Iceberg (native scan) today.

    The run path gates on ``supported_monitor_kinds`` — the runner-advertised
    capability set (#429) — NOT on ``isinstance`` against this Protocol: a
    ``runtime_checkable`` isinstance is a name-only structural match, so an
    unrelated ``run_monitors`` method would pass the gate and then TypeError at
    the call instead of raising the clean unsupported-kind error. Keeping the
    capability data-driven also keeps the monitor-kind seam orthogonal to the
    datasource seam (ADR 0012): new kinds (#592/#593) extend a runner's set,
    never the orchestrator's dispatch.

    One ``CheckOutcome`` per ``MonitorSpec``, in order. A monitor that can't be
    evaluated (bad column, type mismatch) yields an ``errored`` outcome rather
    than failing its siblings — mirroring `CheckRunner` semantics.
    """

    supported_monitor_kinds: frozenset[str]

    def run_monitors('''

content.text: Now the `run_service` gate:

input.new_string:
if monitor_idx:
        # Capability gate (#429): the runner ADVERTISES which monitor kinds it
        # evaluates. Never `isinstance(runner, MonitorRunner)` — a
        # runtime_checkable Protocol matches on the method NAME alone, so an
        # unrelated `run_monitors` would pass the gate and TypeError at the call;
        # and per-kind capability keeps this dispatch data-driven as stateful
        # kinds (#592/#593) land on some runners before others.
        supported = frozenset(getattr(runner, "supported_monitor_kinds", frozenset()))
        unsupported_kinds = sorted({checks[i].kind for i in monitor_idx} - supported)
        if unsupported_kinds:
            raise NotImplementedError(
                f"{type(runner).__name__} does not support monitor kind(s) "
                f"{', '.join(unsupported_kinds)} — these need a monitor-capable "
                "datasource (Snowflake / Unity Catalog / Iceberg)"
            )
        monitor_runner = cast(MonitorRunner, runner)
        monitors = [
            MonitorSpec(kind=checks[i].kind, config=dict(checks[i].config)) for i in monitor_idx
        ]
        monitor_outcomes = monitor_runner.run_monitors(table=table, schema=schema, monitors=monitors)

# author-time gate for freshness/volume checks. The SQL datasources compute the

def test_run_outcomes_rejects_runner_with_unrelated_run_monitors() -> None:
    # The #429 AC: a runner that merely HAS a method named run_monitors (which an
    # isinstance against the runtime_checkable Protocol would have accepted) but
    # advertises no capability is rejected CLEANLY at the gate — never a TypeError
    # from calling an unrelated method.

conda run -n dataq python -m pytest backend/tests -q 2>&1 | tail -1 && git add backend/app backend/tests && git commit -m "refactor(monitors): per-kind strategy registry + runner-advertised capability gate (#726, #429)

The monitor-kind seam (ADR 0012) was extensible in the schema but not the
code: adding a kind meant editing three parallel if-chains in monitors.py
(build_monitor_sql / validate_monitor_config / monitor_outcome), the
authoring allowlist, and the run-path partition — a kind added to one but
not another failed at runtime, not import time.

- monitors.py: the three chains collapse into MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY — one
  MonitorKindStrategy (validate_config / outcome / build_sql) per kind; the
  public function signatures are unchanged. build_sql=None models stateful
  kinds (#592/#593) that have no scalar-SQL form: the SQL path refuses
  cleanly instead of building a wrong query. MONITOR_KINDS derives from the
  registry, so check_service's authoring allowlist and run_service's
  partition widen by registering a kind — nothing else.
- #429: the run path gates on runner-advertised supported_monitor_kinds
  (SF/UC/Iceberg = frozenset(MONITOR_KINDS)) instead of isinstance against
  the runtime_checkable MonitorRunner Protocol — a name-only structural
  match that an unrelated run_monitors method would pass, then TypeError at
  the call. Capability is a per-KIND set, so stateful kinds can land on
  some runners before others without re-entangling the datasource seam.

Tests: registry additivity (a monkeypatched fake kind routes through all
three functions untouched), the no-SQL-form refusal, MONITOR_KINDS
derivation, the #429 impostor (has run_monitors, advertises nothing →
clean NotImplementedError, never a TypeError), and a freshness-only runner
rejecting volume BY NAME. Existing freshness/volume behavior green
unchanged (full suite 2272 passed).

Fixes #726
Fixes #429

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push -u origin refactor/issue-726-monitor-kind-registry && gh pr create --title "refactor(monitors): per-kind strategy registry + capability gate (#726, #429)" --milestone "v1.1 Week 4 — schema_drift monitor kind" --body "## Summary

Fixes #726 and #429 — the last W4 prep before the #592 \`schema_drift\` engine slice, which lands as an additive registry entry + a capability-set widening instead of a fourth copy of the if-chains.

## Changes

- **#726:** \`monitors.py\`'s three parallel kind-chains collapse into \`MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY\` (one \`MonitorKindStrategy\` per kind: \`validate_config\` / \`outcome\` / \`build_sql\`); public signatures unchanged. \`build_sql=None\` models stateful kinds — the SQL path refuses with a clear config error. \`MONITOR_KINDS\` derives from the registry, so \`check_service._V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS\` and the run-path partition follow automatically.
- **#429:** \`_run_outcomes\` gates on runner-advertised \`supported_monitor_kinds\` (declared on SF/UC/Iceberg; the \`MonitorRunner\` Protocol documents it) instead of \`isinstance\` against a \`runtime_checkable\` Protocol (name-only match → TypeError at the call for an impostor). Per-kind capability keeps dispatch data-driven as stateful kinds land unevenly across runners. Stale \`check_service\` comment synced.

## Acceptance criteria

- [x] #726: adding a kind = one registry entry (+ strategy); no chain edits, no \`_V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS\` edit — pinned by the additivity test (fake kind via \`monkeypatch.setitem\`, all three functions route)
- [x] #726: reserved-kind rejection unchanged (unknown kind → \`MonitorConfigError\`; authoring 422 path untouched)
- [x] #726: existing freshness/volume tests green unchanged
- [x] #429: gate no longer isinstance-based; a deliberately non-conforming runner (has \`run_monitors\`, advertises nothing) rejected cleanly in a test
- [x] Bonus pin: a freshness-only runner rejects a volume check **by name**

## Testing

Full backend suite 2272 passed / 97.8% coverage.

Also carries the consolidated docs/progress.md ledger flips (#876/#877/#878/#879 + this PR) in a follow-up commit.

input.prompt:
Code-review finder for repo /Users/arijit/Coding/Python/DataQ, PR #880 (branch refactor/issue-726-monitor-kind-registry vs main; ignore the docs/progress.md commit). Run `git diff main...HEAD`.

Change: monitors.py's three per-kind if-chains (build_monitor_sql / validate_monitor_config / monitor_outcome) collapse into MONITOR_KIND_REGISTRY of MonitorKindStrategy dataclasses; MONITOR_KINDS now derives from the registry; run_service._run_outcomes gates on runner-advertised `supported_monitor_kinds` (declared on SF/UC/Iceberg runners as ClassVar; MonitorRunner Protocol documents the attribute) instead of isinstance.

Hunt for behavior drift and gaps, reading the enclosing code:
(1) Byte-for-byte behavior parity of the extracted _freshness_outcome/_volume_outcome/_freshness_sql/_volume_sql vs the old chain bodies — especially error messages, expectation_type derivation (old used a local f-string with _EXPECTATION_PREFIX; new uses monitor_expectation_type — same?), the order of validation vs None-scalar handling in freshness (old validated column BEFORE checking scalar None — still true?).
(2) MONITOR_KINDS ordering (tuple(dict) insertion order — same ('freshness','volume') as before? anything depend on order?). Any other importer of FRESHNESS/VOLUME/MONITOR_KINDS broken (grep backend/app + frontend catalog generators + backend/tests)?
(3) The gate: `getattr(runner, "supported_monitor_kinds", frozenset())` — any runner class where the attribute exists but is a property/instance attr that could raise? The cast(MonitorRunner, runner) after the gate — is run_monitors guaranteed present when supported_monitor_kinds is non-empty and covers the kinds (an object could advertise kinds but lack the method — is that failure mode acceptable/clean)? Does anything else in the codebase still isinstance(_, MonitorRunner)?
(4) The runtime_checkable MonitorRunner Protocol now has a DATA member (supported_monitor_kinds) — on Python 3.13, isinstance against a runtime_checkable Protocol with a non-method member: does it raise or check hasattr? Is any isinstance call left anywhere that would now break?
(5) check_service authoring path: does it validate kind against _V1_SUPPORTED_KINDS derived from MONITOR_KINDS — confirm nothing else hardcodes ('freshness','volume') (grep string literals in backend/app + frontend catalog JSON fixtures).
(6) The dataclass field name `build_sql: ... | None` — the docstring says stateful kinds refuse; is MonitorConfigError the right error class for 'no SQL form' (callers map it to per-check error / 422)? Trace evaluate_monitors → build_monitor_sql failure handling.

Return up to 6 candidates as JSON [{file, line, summary, failure_scenario}], or []. Final message = ONLY the JSON.

conda run -n dataq python -m pytest backend/tests -q 2>&1 | tail -1 && git add -A && git commit -m "fixup: #880 review — explicit runner capability sets, method check in gate, import-time registry contract

- The self-defeat: runners advertised frozenset(MONITOR_KINDS), so ANY
  future registry entry would auto-advertise on every runner and the
  per-kind gate could never fire — the exact 'stateful kinds land unevenly'
  scenario the gate exists for was structurally impossible. Each runner now
  names EXPLICITLY what it implements ({freshness, volume}), pinned by a
  canary test per runner so widening is a conscious act.
- The mirror hole of the old isinstance gate: an object advertising kinds
  without run_monitors passed the gate and died at the call. The gate now
  also requires a callable run_monitors, rejecting with a clean
  'capability and implementation drifted' NotImplementedError (pinned).
- The registry's registration contract is documented as IMPORT-TIME ONLY
  (a dict-literal entry): every derived value snapshots at import, so a
  runtime mutation would be half-visible (dispatchable but unauthorable).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && git push && HEAD=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
post() { gh api "repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/880/comments" -f commit_id="$HEAD" -f path="$1" -F line="$2" -f side=RIGHT -f body="$3" --jq .id; }
post backend/app/datasources/snowflake.py 180 "**[review — design self-defeat, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** All three runners advertised \`frozenset(MONITOR_KINDS)\` — derived from the registry, not from what the runner implements — so registering the next kind (#592's \`schema_drift\`) would auto-advertise it everywhere and the per-kind gate could never fire: checks would sail past it and die as per-check \`unknown monitor kind\` errors inside the runners. Each runner now names its set explicitly (\`{FRESHNESS, VOLUME}\`), with a canary test per runner making any widening a conscious act."
post backend/app/services/run_service.py 176 "**[review — gate hole, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** The capability gate verified only the data attribute — the mirror image of the isinstance hole it replaced: an object advertising kinds without \`run_monitors\` passed the gate and raised AttributeError at the call. The gate now also requires a callable \`run_monitors\` and rejects with a clean 'capability and implementation drifted' \`NotImplementedError\` (pinned by \`test_run_outcomes_rejects_capability_without_implementation\`)."
post backend/app/datasources/monitors.py 292 "**[review — contract gap, CONFIRMED, fixed in $(git rev-parse --short HEAD)]** \`MONITOR_KINDS\`, the authoring allowlist, and the runners' capability sets all snapshot the registry at import — a runtime registration would be half-visible (dispatchable but unauthorable/unroutable) with no error pointing at the snapshot. The registry comment now states the contract: registration is import-time only (a dict-literal entry, the #592/#593 pattern); tests may monkeypatch for isolation, production code must not."
